business_info: The bank's target segment of customers refers to clients from demographics with higher growth potential, representing the primary growth market for retail banking services.

In internal marketing materials, "target customers" are sometimes described at the household level rather than individual level, but operational reports still tag them only at the client_id level in the client table.

For certain cross-sell analyses, analysts approximate a "growth potential" segment by combining gender, age cohort, and district-level salary bands, but there is no single canonical flag column for this in the current schema.

The term "first days of the month" appears in some legacy campaign briefs and usually referred to the period before the first scheduled payroll run, but the exact cutoff date was left to the discretion of the analyst preparing the report.

When estimating lifetime value, some teams backdate the "account opening" to the earliest customer touchpoint (like a loan pre-approval or a card lead), even if the official account_id was created later in the core system.

In some pilot projects, the bank tested a "target female professionals" offer where target customers were defined as women aged 25–45 living in urban districts, but this segmentation rule was only implemented in off-line campaign lists and not persisted in the data warehouse.

Client gender in the client table is sourced from onboarding forms and may be left blank for legacy records migrated from paper, in which case gender-specific marketing segments cannot be reliably reconstructed historically.

Some retail dashboards group clients into simplified "target" and "non-target" buckets using custom logic in the BI layer; this logic is typically encoded in report formulas rather than as a stored attribute in the source tables.

Branches sometimes record a manual "account start" date in branch logs to track sales performance within a campaign, which can differ from the system-generated dates in the account table by several days.

Client birth_date is stored as the date of birth declared at onboarding and is not updated; analysts derive age bands for segmentation in downstream data marts rather than storing a direct age or age_band field in the client table.

The bank experimented with a "growth potential" scoring model that combined transaction activity, product holdings, and district-level unemployment, but the resulting score was only exposed in a marketing tool and not written back to the core tables.

In historical planning decks, the phrase "primary growth market" was sometimes used interchangeably with "emerging affluent" segments, but those decks did not specify a strict operational rule for assigning clients to that group.

For some campaigns, branch staff were instructed to prioritize women in dual-income households when pitching new accounts, but this behavioral guidance was tracked only through training materials and not encoded in the data model.

District-level attributes such as average salary, unemployment and entrepreneurs per 1,000 inhabitants are updated on an annual basis; analysts often group districts into low/medium/high potential tiers based on these indicators for planning, separate from any client-level segmentation.
Urbanization ratio at the district level is sometimes used as a proxy for market maturity, but it is not part of the formal definition of any specific target segment in current documentation.
Some strategic memos describe the bank's growth strategy as "female-led households in developing districts," but this was more of a narrative positioning than a data-driven rule exposed to analysts.

Many ad-hoc analyses for management simply label the top decile of customers by product revenue as "target" without regard to any demographic criteria, which can conflict with other segmentation approaches used elsewhere.

The requested_date in the account table is populated when the sales officer submits the account application into the core system; if the customer later abandons the process and reapplies, a new account_id and requested_date are created.
Bank_approved_date values can sometimes fall on weekends or public holidays because back-office approvals may be processed in batch overnight, independent of branch opening hours.
Customer_approved_date captures the point when the customer signs or electronically confirms the agreement; for some older products this field may be null because only paper-based evidence existed.

In campaign evaluations, analysts sometimes defined the campaign period as starting a few days before the official launch date to capture early test accounts opened by branch staff, which can create ambiguity around what counts as "in-period".

Month-level analyses sometimes bucket accounts by the month of bank_approved_date, while product penetration studies might instead bucket by customer_approved_date to align with when the customer actually gained access to the product.

Some historical extracts used for modeling have truncated requested_date to the first of each month for anonymization purposes, which can make it difficult to reconstruct day-level opening patterns from those snapshots alone.

The frequency field on the account table captures how often regular statements or settlement cycles occur; although some analysts use it as a rough proxy for account activity, it does not directly encode customer engagement.

OWNER, USER and DISPONENT roles in the disp table are treated equivalently in most portfolio-level analyses, except in regulatory reports where only OWNER relationships are counted as formal account holders.
Each row in the disp table links exactly one client to one account; a single client can appear on multiple accounts and a single account can have multiple clients across different roles.

Some internal scorecards distinguish between "primary" and "secondary" account relationships based on the DISPONENT type, but this distinction is not exposed as a separate field and is inferred only in specific BI reports.

For customer-level analyses, account-to-client mapping is typically constructed by joining disp to account on account_id and then to client on client_id, aggregating across all account roles if no explicit role filter is required.
In some special studies, analysts limit the universe to OWNER relationships only to avoid double-counting accounts with multiple USERS, but this is a methodological choice rather than a system rule.
